title: SoFi Invest Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026)

meta_description: SoFi Invest Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026). SoFi Invest (3.9★, Free). Step-by-step setup, features walkthrough, and pro tips. Updated 2026.
excerpt: Complete guide to setting up and using SoFi Invest — from account creation to pro-level tips.
content:
What is SoFi Invest?

SoFi Invest is a commission-free investment platform owned by SoFi Technologies (founded 2011, headquartered in San Francisco) that lets you trade stocks, ETFs, and options with zero commissions and no account minimums. You can start investing with just $1 in fractional shares and gain access to retail IPOs at their offering price—a feature most brokers reserve for high-net-worth clients. SoFi Invest carries a 3.9/5 rating and works best for beginner investors, buy-and-hold portfolios, and SoFi banking customers who want a fully integrated financial ecosystem. If you value simplicity over advanced tools and want to avoid trading fees entirely, SoFi Invest delivers on that promise.

How to Create Your SoFi Invest Account

Creating a SoFi Invest account takes about 10–15 minutes. Here's what you'll need:

  • Personal information: Full name, date of birth, Social Security Number (SSN)
  • Residency verification: Current address (U.S. residents only)
  • Employment details: Job title, employer, annual income (used for regulatory compliance)
  • Email and phone number: For account access and two-factor authentication
  • Bank account details: Routing number and account number for deposits (optional initially, but required to fund trades)

Step 1: Visit SoFi Invest and start the signup. Go to the SoFi website, tap "Sign Up," and choose whether you want Active Investing, Automated Investing, or both. SoFi will ask if you're already a SoFi Banking customer; existing customers skip identity verification and fund immediately.

Step 2: Complete identity verification. SoFi uses real-time verification (not manual review), so you'll upload a photo ID and take a selfie. If automatic verification fails, you'll receive an email with next steps—this rarely happens if your information matches your ID exactly.

Step 3: Answer suitability questions. SoFi asks about your trading experience, investment goals, and annual income. These questions determine which account types you can access; they don't restrict you from opening the account, but options trading and certain IPO allocations depend on your answers.

Step 4: Link your bank account and deposit. You can start trading immediately, but you need to link a verified bank account to move money in. Use ACH transfers (free, 3–5 business days) or wire transfer ($25 fee, same day). First-time deposits under $10,000 typically clear in 1–2 days; larger deposits may take 3–5.

Setting Up SoFi Invest for the First Time

Once you log in, you'll see the main dashboard. Here's how to configure it:

Dashboard Layout. The home screen shows your portfolio value, today's gain/loss, and a feed of personalized alerts and market news. Left sidebar navigation includes "Invest" (stocks/ETFs/options), "Automated" (robo-advisor), "IPOs" (retail IPO deals), and "Learn" (education center). Unlike competitors like Robinhood or Fidelity, SoFi keeps the layout clean and mobile-first—desktop is functional but secondary.

Enable Alerts. Tap Settings → Notifications → Alerts and turn on price alerts (when a stock hits a target price), earnings alerts, news alerts, and portfolio balance thresholds. Set alerts conservatively; SoFi sends push notifications and emails, and you can customize frequency. This is your first line of defense against missing important moves.

Configure Performance Analytics. SoFi's Analytics tab shows your portfolio breakdown (by sector, asset class, risk level), total return, cost basis, and realized/unrealized gains. Set a preferred time zone and currency (USD default). If you're managing multiple accounts (individual + joint), make sure you're viewing the right one—SoFi consolidates them in the app, but reports track them separately for tax purposes.

Set Your Tax Preference. Go to Account Settings and choose "Tax-Loss Harvesting" if you have Automated Investing. SoFi's robo-advisor automatically sells losing positions to offset gains—this only applies to Automated accounts, not manual trading, but it's worth enabling if tax efficiency matters to you.

Connect Your Bank Account (Duplicate Verification). If you didn't link a bank during signup, do it now under Wallet → Add Bank Account. SoFi uses Plaid (industry standard) and requires two small verification deposits ($0.01 each) that you'll confirm in your bank app—this takes 1–2 days. Once verified, deposits and withdrawals are instant for ACH transfers.

Essential Features You Should Know

1. Commission-Free Trading & Zero Options Contract Fees. Every stock, ETF, and options trade costs $0. This is standard across most retail brokers now, but SoFi's edge is that options orders don't carry per-contract fees (you pay only the spread). If you're buying 10 contracts of a call option, you save $0.65–$1.00 per contract vs. brokers like Tastyworks. For buy-and-hold investors, this doesn't matter; for options traders, it adds up.

2. Retail IPO Access at Offering Price. SoFi offers customers early access to IPOs before they trade publicly. When a company goes public, SoFi allocates shares to active users at the IPO price (say, $25), and you can lock in an allocation before day-one trading begins. This is genuinely rare for a free broker. You won't get IPO allocations every week, but if you're watching a hot IPO, SoFi's customer base often gets priority. Check the "IPOs" tab weekly to see upcoming deals.

3. Fractional Shares & $1 Minimum. You can buy $1 of any stock or ETF, even expensive ones like Berkshire Hathaway or Tesla. This lets beginners build diversified portfolios without waiting to save $500 for a full share. Use fractional shares to round out your portfolio (e.g., own 0.5 shares of SPY to hit a specific dollar amount) or to invest small lump sums consistently. Fractional shares are fully owned, not borrowed, so you receive dividends and voting rights prorated.

4. Automated Investing (Robo-Advisor) with 0% Management Fee. SoFi's "Automated" account is a robo-advisor that builds a portfolio matching your risk profile (conservative, moderate, or aggressive) using low-cost ETFs. It rebalances quarterly at no charge and offers optional tax-loss harvesting. Betterment and Vanguard Personal Advisor charge 0.25–0.30%; SoFi charges nothing. If you're not picking stocks, this is the path of least resistance and one of SoFi's strongest differentiators.

5. Performance Analytics & Portfolio Tracking. The Analytics tab breaks down your portfolio by sector (healthcare, tech, finance), asset class (stocks, ETFs, options), and risk level. You can filter by time period (1 day, 1 month, 1 year, all-time) and compare your performance to the S&P 500 benchmark. SoFi calculates time-weighted returns (how your portfolio performed independent of deposit timing), which is more accurate than dollar-weighted for long-term tracking. Use this to audit your allocation quarterly and spot concentration risk (e.g., if 60% of your portfolio is in tech).

6. Mobile App & News Feed. SoFi's mobile app is slick—one of the best in the broker space for casual investors. It's faster than the desktop site and includes a curated news feed with market stories, earnings updates, and company analysis written by SoFi's editorial team. Tap any stock to see recent news, analyst estimates, and insider activity. The feed is opinionated but balanced; SoFi doesn't promote hype stocks or day-trading narratives.

7. Education Center. SoFi's "Learn" section includes videos on stock picking, ETF investing, options basics, and financial planning. Content ranges from kindergarten-level (what is a stock?) to intermediate (covered calls, sector rotation). It's not a substitute for formal courses, but it's a decent starting point if you're new to investing. Competitors like Fidelity and Schwab have deeper education libraries, but SoFi's content is more visually polished.

SoFi Invest Pricing: Which Plan Should You Choose?

Active Investing: $0/month. Pay nothing to trade stocks, ETFs, and options commission-free with access to retail IPOs, news, and alerts. No subscription, no hidden fees, no account minimum. This is SoFi Invest's bread-and-butter offering and beats every other "free" broker on IPO access alone.

Automated Investing: $0/month. SoFi's robo-advisor charges zero in management fees (0% vs. 0.25–0.30% at competitors). You pay nothing for portfolio building, rebalancing, and optional tax-loss harvesting. You only pay the ETF expense ratios (~0.05–0.15%), which you'd pay anyway with any broker. This is a steal if you want passive investing without a wealth advisor.

SoFi Plus: $10/month (optional upgrade). This subscription adds perks like cashback on debit transactions, APY boost on savings, and life insurance discounts. It's geared toward SoFi's banking customers, not investors specifically. Skip it if you only use SoFi for investing; it doesn't add trading benefits.

Who should choose which plan?

  • Beginners & buy-and-hold investors: Start with Automated Investing ($0). Let SoFi build a diversified portfolio; you don't pick stocks, and you pay zero fees. Come back quarterly to review performance.
  • DIY stock pickers: Use Active Investing ($0). You pick stocks and ETFs; SoFi charges nothing per trade. If you use options, the zero per-contract fee is valuable.
  • Hybrid investors: Split between Active and Automated. Use Automated for core holdings (index funds) and Active for tactical trades (individual stocks). SoFi lets you run both simultaneously in one account.
  • SoFi Banking customers: Consider SoFi Plus ($10/mo) if you already bank with SoFi and want the full ecosystem (checking + investing + lending). It's optional, and pure investing is free.

Pro Tips for Getting the Most Out of SoFi Invest

  • Set up recurring ACH deposits to dollar-cost-average. Use Settings → Transfers to automate weekly or monthly deposits. SoFi's UI makes it easy to set a fixed amount and schedule; most brokers bury this feature. Automation removes emotion and enforces discipline.
  • Monitor the IPO allocation tab weekly. SoFi releases new IPO allocations on the IPO tab, and slots fill fast if a deal is hot. You don't need to commit; just tap to "Express Interest" and SoFi reserves shares. Withdrawing interest is penalty-free if you change your mind.
  • Use limit orders instead of market orders for options. SoFi's options flow is retail-focused, which means liquidity can be thin on far-dated or out-of-the-money contracts. A market order on a 50-cent bid-ask spread will hurt; place a limit order at the midpoint and wait 30 seconds. This is less critical for at-the-money calls/puts on large-cap stocks, but it saves money on spreads.
  • Combine fractional shares with round-number thinking. Instead of owning 47.3 shares of an ETF, use fractional shares to hold exactly $10,000 of the ETF (which might be 42.7 shares). This simplifies portfolio math and makes rebalancing less awkward. SoFi's fractional share math is transparent, so you always know your exact cost basis.
  • Leverage tax-loss harvesting in Automated accounts once a year. Review your Automated account in November/December. If the market's down, the robo-advisor will have sold losers to offset gains. Check the "Transactions" tab to confirm tax-loss harvesting was applied; if it wasn't, enable it in Settings and manually trigger a rebalance.
  • Use performance analytics to catch sector creep. SoFi's Analytics tab shows your sector exposure. If you own individual tech stocks plus a tech-heavy index fund, you might be 70% tech without realizing it. Review sector breakdown quarterly and rebalance if any single sector exceeds 30–40% of your portfolio.
  • Check insider activity on stocks you own. Tap any stock in SoFi, scroll to "Insider Activity," and see if company insiders are buying or selling. If insiders are dumping shares, it's a yellow flag (though not a sell signal alone). SoFi sources this data from SEC filings, so it's delayed by 1–2 weeks—don't over-trade on it, but use it for conviction checks on existing positions.

Common SoFi Invest Issues and How to Fix Them

Issue 1: ACH Deposit Stuck in "Pending" Status. SoFi shows deposits as pending until your bank clears them. If it's been more than 5 business days, check your bank's end first (open the SoFi Plaid connection in your bank's app and verify SoFi is authorized). If Plaid is connected, email SoFi support with your bank name and transfer reference number. Rarely, a bank blocks SoFi; if yours does, use a wire transfer ($25 fee, immediate) or switch banks.

Issue 2: Can't See IPO Allocation Emails. SoFi sends IPO notifications to both your registered email and the app's notification center. Check Settings → Notifications → Email and enable "IPO Alerts." IPO slots typically fill within 12–24 hours, so if you miss an email, you've missed the allocation. Add IPO notifications to your phone's VIP inbox so they don't get filtered.

Issue 3: Fractional Shares Selling at Unexpected Prices. When you sell fractional shares, SoFi executes the order at the market price during market hours. If you sell after-hours or during a volatile open, the fill price might be worse than the last quoted price. Solution: place a limit sell order with a price target instead of selling at market. This requires patience (your order might not fill immediately), but it prevents slippage on thin shares.

Issue 4: Customer Support Takes Days to Respond. SoFi's support is chat-first (available 8 AM–8 PM ET weekdays). Chat is fast for account setup questions but slow for complex issues (e.g., tax forms, missing dividends, account transfers). If chat can't help, request a callback or escalate to email—prepare to wait 24–48 hours. For urgent issues, call during business hours; support numbers are in the app under Help.

Is SoFi Invest Worth It? Our Verdict

SoFi Invest is worth using if you want free, frictionless investing with rare IPO access and no account minimums. The 3.9/5 rating reflects strong execution on basics (trades, transfers, mobile app) paired with weak support for power users (no advanced charting, no Level 2 data, no futures). If you're a beginner, buy-and-hold investor, or robo-advisor fan, SoFi's zero fees and automated features are hard to beat. If you're an active trader who needs screeners, technical analysis, or options depth, check out Tastyworks or Interactive Brokers instead. Compare SoFi to Fidelity and Robinhood if you're torn—all three are free, but SoFi's IPO edge and robo-advisor are unique. Read the full SoFi Invest review for deeper feature breakdowns and user feedback.
